questions.text: The process of breakdown of food in the cell with the release of energy is called:
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration takes place in the cells of all organisms to release energy from glucose.

questions.text: When breakdown of glucose occurs with the use of oxygen, it is called:
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen to break down glucose into carbon dioxide and water.

questions.text: Organisms like yeast that can survive in the absence of air are called:
questions.explanation: Anaerobes get energy through anaerobic respiration which does not require oxygen.

questions.text: In anaerobic respiration, glucose breaks down into:
questions.explanation: Yeasts convert glucose into alcohol and carbon dioxide in the absence of oxygen.

questions.text: During heavy exercise, we get cramps in the legs due to the accumulation of:
questions.explanation: Partial breakdown of glucose in muscles during heavy exercise produces lactic acid, causing cramps.

questions.text: The number of times a person breathes in a minute is termed as the:
questions.explanation: On average, an adult human at rest breathes 15–18 times per minute.

questions.text: Taking in of air rich in oxygen into the body is called:
questions.explanation: Inhalation is the process of breathing in air containing oxygen.

questions.text: Which part of the respiratory system is also known as the windpipe?
questions.explanation: The trachea is the tube that connects the pharynx and larynx to the lungs.

questions.text: A large, muscular sheet which forms the floor of the chest cavity is called:
questions.explanation: Breathing involves the movement of the diaphragm and the rib cage.

questions.text: During inhalation, the diaphragm moves:
questions.explanation: During inhalation, the ribs move up and out and the diaphragm moves down to increase space in the chest cavity.

questions.text: During exhalation, the ribs move:
questions.explanation: Exhalation involves the ribs moving down and inwards while the diaphragm moves up.

questions.text: The percentage of oxygen in exhaled air is approximately:
questions.explanation: Inhaled air has 21% oxygen, while exhaled air has about 16.4% oxygen.

questions.text: Cockroaches breathe through small openings on the sides of their body called:
questions.explanation: Spiracles are the external openings that lead into a network of air tubes (tracheae).

questions.text: The network of air tubes in insects for gas exchange is called:
questions.explanation: Tracheae are used to transport oxygen directly to the tissues in insects.

questions.text: Earthworms breathe through their:
questions.explanation: The skin of an earthworm is moist and slimy, allowing gases to pass through easily.

questions.text: Frogs can breathe through their lungs and also through their:
questions.explanation: Frogs have a pair of lungs but can also respire through their moist skin.

questions.text: Which organs help fish to use oxygen dissolved in water?
questions.explanation: Gills help fish to extract dissolved oxygen from water efficiently.

questions.text: What is the end product of glucose breakdown in muscle cells during anaerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: In muscle cells, partial breakdown of glucose results in lactic acid.

questions.text: Which of these is used to test the presence of CO2 in exhaled air?
questions.explanation: Exhaled air turns lime water milky due to the presence of carbon dioxide.

questions.text: The process of respiration in plants occurs during:
questions.explanation: Like animals, plants respire continuously throughout the day and night.

questions.text: Which part of the plant takes in oxygen from the air spaces in the soil?
questions.explanation: Root hairs take in oxygen from the soil particles.

questions.text: The exchange of gases in leaves occurs through:
questions.explanation: Stomata are tiny pores on the surface of leaves for gas exchange.

questions.text: When we exhale, the air is rich in:
questions.explanation: Respiration produces carbon dioxide which is expelled during exhalation.

questions.text: The capacity of lungs can be measured using:
questions.explanation: A spirometer is an instrument used to measure the volume of air inspired and expired by the lungs.

questions.text: Which of the following organisms is an anaerobe?
questions.explanation: Yeast can survive without oxygen and produces alcohol via fermentation.

questions.text: Increased physical activity leads to a/an ________ in breathing rate.
questions.explanation: More activity requires more energy, thus more oxygen and faster breathing.

questions.text: During exhalation, the diaphragm moves:
questions.explanation: The diaphragm moves back to its original relaxed position (upwards) during exhalation.

questions.text: Insects have a network of air tubes called ________ for gas exchange.
questions.explanation: Tracheae are the tubes; spiracles are the openings.

questions.text: Why do we sneeze when we inhale dust?
questions.explanation: Sneezing is a protective mechanism to remove irritants from the nasal cavity.

questions.text: Which gas is used during aerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration requires oxygen to break down food.

questions.text: The small air sacs at the end of the bronchioles in lungs are:
questions.explanation: Alveoli are the primary sites for gas exchange in the human lungs.

questions.text: Fish gills are supplied with ________ for gas exchange.
questions.explanation: Blood vessels in the gills absorb oxygen from the water.

questions.text: Which process allows roots to take in air?
questions.explanation: Gases move into and out of root cells by diffusion.

questions.text: Lenticels are found in which part of the plant for respiration?
questions.explanation: Lenticels are pores in the bark of woody stems for gas exchange.

questions.text: The accumulation of ________ causes muscle fatigue.
questions.explanation: Lactic acid builds up during anaerobic respiration in muscles.

questions.text: Respiration is an ________ reaction.
questions.explanation: Respiration releases energy, making it an exothermic biological process.

questions.text: Whales and dolphins breathe through:
questions.explanation: They are mammals and breathe air through blowholes located on the top of their heads.

questions.text: The percent of CO2 in inhaled air is about:
questions.explanation: Atmospheric air contains approximately 0.04% carbon dioxide.

questions.text: The percent of CO2 in exhaled air is about:
questions.explanation: Exhaled air has a higher concentration of CO2, roughly 4.4%.

questions.text: What happens to our breathing when we feel sleepy or drowsy?
questions.explanation: Our breathing rate slows down when the body is at rest or feeling drowsy.
